item.title: A little less irrational, then maybe a little more.
item.content:encoded: I snapped off the bit at the end, since I don't really see the communication angle going anywhere. Maybe I could slap it back in later, with the result being only disappointing gibberish. Also considering making it so that every irrational number is one of these entities, but that it doesn't quite exist until we calculate it or something, or some kind of quantum pattern screamer nonsense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How, out of all the many things wrong with the SCP, was *that* the one that got missed? Thank you for finding it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In Footnote 1, SCP-4313 is referenced, when I believe this should be the titular 4134.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It might be my background speaking, but I don't find the concept here compelling at all.

There's plenty of weird shit in mathematics: the monster group, the axiom of choice, Ramsey numbers, the whole of category theory… Given the sheer amount of material you can draw from, the fact that this piece goes for the most obvious "irrational number has messages hidden it" angle feels like a let down.

There are some nitpicks, like writing out a huge number multiple times instead of abbreviating it, and somehow using both binary (= base-2) and base-5 at the same time. But the concept itself is the thing I'm most concerned about.

item.title: Re: Feedback
item.content:encoded:
I feel like I've seen pi calculated to more digits than referenced, right? Maybe make it more like .. Well idk how to get black boxes so pretend redacted is black boxes,
"(Redacted) x10(to the power of redacted several digits)"

item.title: Good article!
item.content:encoded:
As a fellow math fan, I LOVED this article. The idea of an ancient race fundamentally altering the universe to leave a message for a future race is genius. I kept mulling over HOW they would encode it and yeah, you would have to fundamentally alter logic itself.

Quick note: the letter by the researcher was great! I think have the two additional addendums and a [DATA EXPUNGED] afterward breaks the flow. I think that you should leave the letter to be at the end. Also, I think you could remove the data-expungement. It doesn’t really add anything, because there is no “terror factor” to expunge.

Overall, awesome work! I loved thinking about the “mathematical philosophical” implications of it. Just could use a little polishinng at the end there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I normally dont care for math based scps but something about this just… spoke to me. +1 for a very well crafted article.

item.title: I like this
item.content:encoded: Of course, I hope you add a section explaining just what they found in the digits of e that are so redaction-worthy, and I hope you add more text that they "decoded" from pi. Still, +1 for such a nice concept.

item.title: SCPi? Seriously?
item.content:encoded:
To be honest, I am not against numbers with anomalous qualities, but this is one of the universal constants we're talking about, and to make that an SCP does not appeal to the number person like me. I'm sorry to say this, but I'd pick the nominated-for-deletion infinite storage Samsung phone over this.

Downvoted.

Edit: Seeing that it's not just pi that is anomalous, but that e, phi ((1+sqrt(5))/2) and sqrt(2) are anomalous as well, I'll say no vote. If all square roots of prime numbers can be converted to share this anomaly, I'll give you a +1.

item.title: Re: Typo
item.content:encoded: Typo addressed; thank you!

item.title: Typo
item.content:encoded:
In Dr. King's note, there appears to be an unintentional typo.

a bottomless staircase or and endless IKEA

I believe you mean /an/ endless IKEA.

I wish this article grabbed me more than it does. As a math student, I have thought about Pi and e for a while and I wish the reasons why we're gravitated towards irrational numbers weren't explained to me by Dr. King in such plain language.

item.title: Re: behold the l o n g p o s t
item.content:encoded:
Guess I’m also clueless about how binary works. I guess some kind of base seven would work best what with the second hytoth and all. But I’ve worked a lot this and I think that, beyond minor edits, there’s not much more I can do for it. What we have here as a newbie author who overstepped his capabilities out of eagerness to bring his ideas to life. I still think I did pretty good for a 2nd SCP, all things considered, but if she sinks she sinks.
That said, Pi the Pattern Screamer doesn’t make it, I’d be happy to collaborate on a 2.0 in the future. I’d really like to see this done right someday.
Thank you for all your help.

item.title: behold the l o n g p o s t
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
- l o n g

I think that anybody educated enough to work at the Foundation knows what pi is, as do most readers. While defining it more specifically would make the document more scientific in tone, I'm afraid it might come across as filler or even patronizing. Besides, I wanted to deliver the same kind of impact that comes from "SCP-001 is the designation given to the Sun" and the other SCPs that do the same thing, where the first sentence of the description outright tells you that the SCP is actually something we thought was normal

This is an admirable goal, and something I try to do a lot in my writing as well — I fully support you trying the same thing! But, realistically, you lose accuracy (and therefore realism) because of it. Perhaps something like "SCP-4314 is the irrational constant pi (π)1." would preserve the best of both?

a nonexistent quantum sentience has no reason to count by tens, but if there's anything a pattern screamer understands it's the "is/is not" nature of binary. Also, the message isn't (necessarily) intended for humans specifically. The screamers want alien races with a 5-digit numerical system to be able to understand this too

I think you're confusing base-2 with just "ones and zeros in base ten". I'll try to explain:

4.101 is a valid base-10 number, equal to 4(1) + 1(1/10) + 0(1/100) + 1(1/1000). This does not mean it is binary, or even that the decimal part is binary. If we convert it into binary form, we get… well, 100.0001100111011011001. A completely different number. And in base-5 (the five-digit alien system you mentioned), it's 4.022303030303… forever. Just because it's binary in base-10, doesn't mean it's base-2, and it doesn't mean it's binary in any other base. The fact that it is binary in base-10 implies it's intended for us much more than, say, having it give a message when converted into base-2.

Yes, it's overly poetic but so were most of the top-scoring 3000 contest entries

This is a good point, but both 3000 and 3001 had massive amounts of clinical buildup and character work before the poetic aspects started creeping in. And, even when they do turn up, there's a narrative reason for them to be there. In 3001, it's because it's the recording of the event itself. For 3000, it's more direct — it doesn't exist to further the story, it is the story. What you convey through the end-note here isn't actually a key point of the narrative, you're just telling the reader how they should be interpreting it. A key narrative rule to remember is to show, not tell. 3000 gets away with it because of the sheer quality of writing, and the combined efforts of three established authors. This article does not.

I think I implied pretty heavily that all irrational numbers contain similar messages. I tried outright stating that was the case in earlier versions of this thing, but that didn't go over well so I left it up to the reader's imagination instead.

That's not really the problem I was talking about. The implications come through fine; they're just… not particularly interesting. There are other messages similar to this one? Cool, I guess. It doesn't have any bearing on the real world, so I'm not sure whether it's supposed to be a bad thing or not. And, at the end of the day, you've just expunged it, so I'm left wondering why they expunged it. What message could be so awful that the Foundation has to strip it from their database? There's no lead-up to the e-message, and nothing to imply its contents other than "it exists and it's similar to the pi-message".

I don't really understand the math in question well enough to do it justice, although I doubt that that most readers would either.

Key difference here. It's always a good idea to know more about a topic than your readers, because it's important that they feel like the author knows what they're talking about. Whether it's researching lipid chemistry or Chinese history (both subjects I know certain authors have delved into in the pursuit of SCPs), you should have a good grasp on the subject matter. It doesn't have to be perfect, but a skim of a wikipedia article or two is never a bad idea!

I might just offer it up to anybody who wants a crack at the base concept

At some point, if nobody else comes forward, I might take you up on that offer! I'd be willing to collaborate with you on a revamped version at some point, if you'd be up for that sort of thing — it might have to wait a while, though, since real life is pretty hectic at the moment for me.

Solve for bear is hilarious, by the way.

Thanks :)

Footnotes
1. Equal to the ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter.

item.title: Re: Good Framework, could use more narrative
item.content:encoded: I tried dangerous a couple different ways. First I made it so the messages and thus the value of pi changed when measured but that was mathematically bogus. Then I had the square root of seven making threats but in the absence of the tale that would actually elaborate on that it kind of fell flat. I’ll add it back in when the tale is finished. Hopefully it will go over better then.

item.title: Good Framework, could use more narrative
item.content:encoded: I love the concept, but its effectively just the introduction to a concept as it stands. Give it some threat, give it some setting, make it tangible and dangerous, or heartwarming. Whatever you make it, if you make it more, you'll have a +1 from me. Abstaining until then. Hope to see more!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1 - I think that anybody educated enough to work at the Foundation knows what pi is, as do most readers. While defining it more specifically would make the document more scientific in tone, I'm afraid it might come across as filler or even patronizing. Besides, I wanted to deliver the same kind of impact that comes from "SCP-001 is the designation given to the Sun" and the other SCPs that do the same thing, where the first sentence of the description outright tells you that the SCP is actually something we thought was normal. I guess it doesn't have the same punch since it wasn't prefaced by any real suspense, but I'm happy with it.
2 - I went with binary because pattern screamers also screw around with computers in SCP-000. SCP-S, and SCP-2173. It's also the simplest option; a nonexistent quantum sentience has no reason to count by tens, but if there's anything a pattern screamer understands it's the "is/is not" nature of binary. Also, the message isn't (necessarily) intended for humans specifically. The screamers want alien races with a 5-digit numerical system to be able to understand this too. Going with binary, the most basic option, is the best way to do that, I think.
3 - Yes, it's overly poetic but so were most of the top-scoring 3000 contest entries (not that this is anywhere close to as good as the Big Eel or Red Reality). My point is, I've seen plenty of examples where the clinical tone is discarded in favor of the narrative, and I don't personally have a problem with it if it's controlled to a single addendum or some other kind of sub-document. I tried a few different interpretations, and I've decided that this really is the best way to convey the concept I'm trying to get across. Describing the inherently strange nature of irrational numbers doesn't work in a clinical tone, except maybe for advanced mathematicians.
4 - The [DATA EXPUNGED] follows Dr. King noting that it talks in the plural, and an attempt to calculate the value of e. I think I implied pretty heavily that all irrational numbers contain similar messages. I tried outright stating that was the case in earlier versions of this thing, but that didn't go over well so I left it up to the reader's imagination instead. I guess it didn't work for you, but I'm not sure there's anything I could do to fix that.

Maybe I'm just the wrong person to write this. Lots of people have told me that this a good idea with poor execution, and I think that's probably because I don't really understand the math in question well enough to do it justice, although I doubt that that most readers would either. If the poor thing gets deleted, I might just offer it up to anybody who wants a crack at the base concept ("pi is a pattern screamer") and see what happens.

Edit: Solve for bear is hilarious, by the way. When I mentioned it earlier, I did not intend to give the impression that I don't like it, if that's what you got.

item.title: Re: Bad Math
item.content:encoded: That'd be a massive amount of articles to downvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting concept, poorly executed. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A few problems I noticed with this:

  1. You say "SCP-4314 is the irrational number defined as pi" but never actually define it yourself. I could say 'for the context of the following equations, the symbol π represents the irrational quantity sqrt(2)' and that'd be just as mathematically valid, if not a little confusing. You should specify it's the ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter.
  2. You introduce binary for no apparent reason. Any system of digits can be translated into base-26, and then to English: I see no reason why Ortothan would be any different. It just seems weird to limit yourself to two digits rather than 10.
  3. That note is overly-poetic as all hell and has no place in a scientific document. It comes off as a poor attempt to be profound, and just beats the reader over the head with 'LOOK HOW COOL AND SPOOKY IT IS'.
  4. "[DATA EXPUNGED]" is an annoying story cop-out when there's no evidence there's any more story to be expunged.

As a lot of other people have said, I really do like this concept. It's not entirely original, but there's still a lot of story potential here without you needing to resort to pseudo-profound statements about irrational numbers. Solve For Bear works because I took a base concept and explored the nooks and crannies of it1, which I think you need to do here — commit to the concept, rather than just leaving it airy and mysterious. Mathematical anomalies, in my experience, tend to require a certain level of detail to them that's hard to carry out without being overly-complex or boring. There's a lot of good surreal horror that could be gotten from this.

No-vote, because I'd like to see this succeed in some form or another.

Footnotes
1. Admittedly I tried to make it humorous, which always helps suspension of disbelief.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Only one person (thanks, MathBrush) reviewed it in my Sandbox over the ~week it was there, so I went ahead and posted it. I've been editing the crap out of it to keep out of the deletion threshold. If that's not the correct procedure, I'd be happy to delete and repost. the only reason I didn't do it already is because I thought it would be frowned upon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's what the sandbox is for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this quite a bit. Based on these comments it seems like you've improved it a lot. You should delete and repost so people give it a second look.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have officially removed the arbitrary evulz.

item.title: Re: Nearly there..
item.content:encoded: Done. Let’s get this baby back to -4.

item.title: Nearly there..
item.content:encoded: Okay, you've certainly improved it. Its a decent read, except for one glaring issue. "though termination is to be used only as a last resort to avoid suspicion." Just no. You've already said you can use amnestics, people who've calculated it past this point, and termination doesn't really avoid suspicion. Also, the foundation does not kill innocent people unless absolutely necessary. They may do dubious things with D class personnel for the greater good, but this really paints them in the wrong light and doesn't fit with the canon at all. Remove that, and I think it deserves a +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spoiler crit
close

Ok I'm in agreement here that you have something potentially cool here, though I'm not sure cutting down on it is a good idea.

Whether math is a fundamental part of nature, creation of man, a combination of both or something else entirely is highly debated and great fuel for sci-fi and I personally think exploring commonly accepted laws such as math as being extra-natural is rather untapped1

Once you establish that though it's just kinda that. Sure one of them is apparently evil and powerful but that's about it. There's so much more you can do with this: Where did these things come from? How do they observe reality? That kind of thing. I'm not saying not to be vague but don't just have a base concept either.

It also needs to be polished an re-worded but that's not my thing.

Footnotes
1. I'm rather fond on the theory that MEKHANE made mathematics.

item.title: Re: Bad Math
item.content:encoded: trippen

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding this wholeheartedly. I think this suffers from a lack of clear focus. The initial premise feels like a good topic to explore - moving into the realm of something just beyond what we can typocally perceive/know of, where there is some form of breakdown of logic, or something secret, or horrid, laying just beyond the cusp of our understanding of the universe.

However, as it progresses it seems to neglect the KISS (keep ir simple, stupid) principle, which somewhat weakens the horror with excessive complexity, and strains disbelief by introducing both A) that each time this area of pi is reached, we find a different message and B) that there are other numbers with their own consiousness behind them.

Either of those things, sure, may make a fine story in their own right, and hell, maybe all those elements can be better threaded in a more coherent manner that is able to satisfy. As-is, I don't get the sense that these traits, their discoveries flow well, and ultimately, I'm turned off to it all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh. I don’t really know that much about coding (in case you couldn’t tell). When my classes are done for the day I’ll make that adjustment. I might also scrap everything after “all irrational numbers are considered instances of SCP-4314”, at least until I’ve written the tale that justifies it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's possible to translate any countable set of symbols into binary (or any other numerical base), including alphabets.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They appear in the language of whoever’s doing the calculation, but Ortothan is actually a better idea. Would it be possible to convert from binary to Ortothan, though?

I haven’t read Contact, what does it say about pi?

Never mind, I looked it up and now I feel like crap for unintentionally ripping off St. Carl.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Similar to the thing about pi from the end of Contact, not that comparison to Carl Sagan is remotely fair.

I think this is either too much or too little. The messages are just kind of there and feel disconnected. Also having them be in English seems far too convenient and feels unsupported (why not Ortothan, given the content?).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Try the mathematical tag.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd have much preferred this if it had ended about halfway through. I don't mind Pi getting anomalous after some point or even the fact that it somehow isn't a constant (that'd seriously break a lot of number theory), but at some point it veers way too hard into mathematical woo territory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What SCPs are you taking about? I’m only familiar with one numerical skip, and I’m pretty sure I didn’t summon any grizzly bears

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay I expanded Dr King’s note a lot, hopefully that’ll add some horror to all this. I’ll collapse it later, right now I’m editing from my phone and can’t make the collapsible work properly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are a lot of SCPs that are numbers, but in addition to them being confusing, a lot of them are kind of bland. Fir the most part if you see one you’ve seen them all. Sorry it’s a good idea but I have to downvote

item.title: Re: Feedback
item.content:encoded: Yes, but he’s right. I should provide a little more than “look, pattern screamers, weird messages”

item.title: Re: Feedback
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I’m not a mathematician so I had to bs it a bit. If you have any suggestions as to how I could make it more plausible I welcome them. And I’ll try to add to the narrative some as well. I’m already planning to link a tale to it, one that I’m going to write as soon as I’m done with the big calculus test and the informational speech I have this week. In the meantime, I might expand Dr. King’s note to give the audience a bit more context.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i absolutely love the pattern screamer lore and everything tied to it and this fits the frame perfectly in the sense people would have had to intensely search for such an anomaly and the page, description, addendums and idea are all perfect yet unlike other pattern screamer scp's it has very little build up or gut wrenching flair and is overall great but far too short for a proper pattern screamer entry. +1

item.title: Re: Feedback
item.content:encoded:
(Perhaps explain to us what these creatures are, where they came from, what they're doing what their motivations are, why the foundation needs to hide this information etc.)

Aren't they pattern screamers?

item.title: Feedback
item.content:encoded: Okay so, there's a pretty neat idea in here. I think the concept of pi being an scp is intriguing, and I certainly like how there's a secret hidden within the later digits, so the foundation makes sure people don't work it out that far. However there's two main issues I find with this article. 1. The maths is a bit weird. I don't particularly like the mathematical explanations you've come up with. 2. Whilst the initial idea is very interesting, the article doesn't retain my interest as I'm reading it. I'm still completely clueless as to what this means, aside from "Someone somewhere put a message in this". You as the author may know what that means, but I really don't. You need a more interesting narrative in your article (Perhaps explain to us what these creatures are, where they came from, what they're doing what their motivations are, why the foundation needs to hide this information etc.) So in conclusion; you've got a really good base concept, and I'm certainly intrigued to read more, so touch up on the maths and give me a more engaging narrative. -1 for now

item.title: Re: Bad Math
item.content:encoded: You downvote all articles that aren't about probability functions?

item.title: Re: Bad Math
item.content:encoded: I don't follow, care to elaborate what did you mean with not probabilistic?

item.title: Bad Math
item.content:encoded: Sorry, gotta downvote because it's not probabilistic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: [facepalm]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey man, you need to add a rating module.
